BANGOURA v. USF [SFSC CGC-12-518990|— USF’S MPA IN OPPOSITION TO PLAINTIFF SORIBA }
BANGOURA’S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT

1 IL. INTRODUCTION
2 Defendant University of San Francisco (“the University”) opposes the motion of |
3 || Plaintiff Soriba Bangoura (“Plaintiff”) for an Order to grant him leave of Court to file a First
4 || Amended Complaint, because Plaintiff failed to give sufficient notice of sixteen (16) court days
5 || for this motion. Thus, this motion should be denied. |
6 Moreover, the amendment is wholly unnecessary. Plaintiff pleads in his proposed
7 |/amended complaint the same facts and causes of action that he did in his original Complaint.
8 || Thus, Plaintiff's proposed amended complaint does not plead any new or different causes of
9 || action or damages. The Court should deny the motion for this additional reason.
10 Il. STATEMENT OF FACTS
11 A. Summary Of The Case
12 This is a higher education case. Plaintiff is a former student of the University’s program
13 || of Masters of Science in Web Science with an emphasis in Entrepreneurship. Declaration of
14 || Stacey L. Leask in Support of Defendant University of San Francisco’s Opposition to Plaintiff's
15 || Motion for Leave to File a First Amended Complaint (“Decl. Leask”), 2.
16 Plaintiff was academically disqualified from the program after three semesters for failing
17 || to meet the academic requirements of the program. (Decl. Leask, 2.)
18 B. Procedural Posture And History
19 This matter has been set for trial for May 13, 2013. Written discovery has been
20 ||completed. The University took Plaintiff's deposition. Pending now before the Court, is the
21 || University’s motion for summary judgment, which will be heard on April 4, 2013. (Decl.
22 || Leask, 93.)
23 More specifically, the procedural history of this case is as follows:
24 Proceeding in pro per, Plaintiff filed his Complaint against the University on March 8,
| 25 |}2012. (Decl. Leask, 94, Ex. A.) On April 13, 2012, the University served and filed its Answer
| 26 || to the Complaint. (Decl. Leask, §5, Ex. B.)
27 On August 10, 2012, this Court set the matter for trial for May 13, 2013. (Decl. Leask,
| 28 || 46.)
~ BANGOURA v. USF [SFSC CGC-12-518990}- USF’S MPA IN OPPOSITION TO PLAINTIFF SORIBA _
BANGOURA’S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT
S:\Client\101 USF\050 Bangoura SFSC\Pleadings\Pif's Second Motion for Leave to Amend\FINAL Opposition MT Amend Complaint.doc -I-

 
@ e
1 On January 16, 2013, the University served and filed a Motion for Summary Judgment
2 ||and/or Summary Adjudication (hereinafter “motion for summary judgment”), which is set to be
3 || heard on April 4, 2013, at 9:30 a.m., before the Honorable Judge Marla J. Miller. (Decl. Leask, |
4 147.)
5 The University’s motion for summary judgment asks this Court to dispose of Plaintiff's
6 || Complaint in its entirety and enter final judgment for the University, on the grounds that there is
7 |{no triable issue of material fact as to the claims (if any) alleged in Plaintiff's Complaint. (Decl.
8 || Leask, 98.)
9 On February 26, 2013, Plaintiff filed a motion “for leave of Court to file a first amended
10 ||complaint.” (Decl. Leask, §9.) Plaintiff, however, filed his motion with less than sixteen (16)
11 || court days before the date on which the motion was set to be heard by the Court. (Decl. Leask,
12 || 99, Ex. C.)
13 The Court thus took Plaintiff's motion off calendar, because “the motion was not timely
14 || filed or served under CCP § 1005.” (Decl. Leask, 410, Ex. C.)
15 Plaintiff again has moved this Court for leave of Court to file a first amended complaint.
16 || Plaintiff again has filed and served his motion with less than sixteen (16) court days before the
17 || date on which this motion is set to be heard by the Court (March 25, 2013). (Decl. Leask, J11-
18 {| 12, Ex. D.)
19 Plaintiff also did not specify the purpose and effect of the proposed amendments, why
20 {| the proposed amendments are necessary and proper; when the facts giving rise to the proposed |
21 |{amendment were discovered; and the reasons why the request for amendment was not made |
22 || earlier. (Decl. Leask, 414.) |
23 The only notable difference between the proposed First Amended Complaint (“FAC”) |
24 || and the original Complaint is that Plaintiff's proposed FAC is presented on pleading paper and
25 ||sets forth allegations under paragraph numbers, whereas the original Complaint did not use
26 || headings or paragraph numbers. (Decl. Leask, 45, Exs. A, D.)
27 |I\\
28 || \\\
~ BANGOURA v. USF [SFSC CGC-12-518990|- USFS MPA IN OPPOSITION TO PLAINTIFF SORIBA _
BANGOURA’S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT
S:\Client\101 USF1050 Bangoura SFSC\Pleadings\Pi's Second Motion for Leave to Amend\FINAL Opposition MT Amend Complaint.doc -2-

 
1 Otherwise, there are no substantive changes to what Plaintiff possibly has alleged as
2 || causes of action in his narrative complaint.' (Decl. Leask, 4/16, Exs. A, D.)
3 Like his original Complaint, in the proposed FAC, Plaintiff pleads facts in a narrative
4 ||fashion. Although slightly different in form, Plaintiff alleges no new material facts in his
5 || Complaint. Accordingly, the potential causes of action are the same as those fairly identified in
| 6 || Plaintiff's original Complaint. In no manner does the new pleading add a new cause of action
| 7 || based on different legal theories. (Decl. Leask, {9 15-19, Exs. A, D.)
8 Plaintiff also does not add any new defendants. Like his original Complaint, Plaintiff's
9 || proposed FAC names only the University as a defendant in this action. (Decl. Leask, 20, Exs. |
10 ||A,D.)
11 Further, Plaintiff makes no change in the damages that he claims. In his proposed FAC,
12 || Plaintiff prays for the same damages as he did in his original Complaint. Although Plaintiff
13 || now specifies that he prays for damages “in an amount in excess of US $30,150,000”, this figure
14 || does not increase the amount of damages prayed for in the original Complaint. (Decl. Leask,
15 || 21, Exs. A, D.)
16 Thus, there are no substantive changes to parties, causes of action or damages alleged in
17 || Plaintiff's proposed FAC.
18 Ill, ANALYSIS AND ARGUMENT
19 A. Plaintiff Failed To Give Sufficient Notice For This Motion And Thus
30 The Motion Should Be Denied
41 California Code of Civil Procedure section 472 states that:
“Any pleading may be amended once by the party of course, and without costs, at
22 any time before the answer or demurrer is filed, or after demurrer and before the
trial of the issue of law thereon, by filing the same as amended and serving a copy |
23 of the adverse party...”
24 California Code of Civil Procedure section 473(a)(1) states that:
25
26 ' Plaintiff's original Complaint and proposed FAC fails to identify causes of action by number or by title or types.
The University has fairly attempted to identify each of the possible causes of action in the narrative as follows: (1)
7 Discrimination and/or Harassment under Title V1, 42 U.S.C. §2000d, et seq., and/or California’s Unruh Act; Cal
Civil Code §51, et seq., ; (2) Breach of Contract; (3) Misrepresentation; (4) Intentional Infliction of Emotional
28 Distress; (5) Invasion of Privacy; and (6) Conversion. (Decl. Leask, 919, Exs. A, D.)
BANGOURA vy. USF [SFSC CGC-12-518990]— USF’S MPA IN OPPOSITION TO PLAINTIFF SORIBA
BANGOURA’S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT
S:\Client\101 USF\050 Bangoura SFSC\Pleadings\Pif's Second Motion for Leave to Amend\FINAL Opposition MT Amend Complaint.doc -3-

 
1 “The court may, in furtherance of justice, and on any terms as may be proper,
allow a party to amend any pleading or proceeding by adding or striking out the
2 name of any party, or by correcting a mistake in the name of a party, or a mistake
in any other respect; and may, upon like terms, englarge the time for answer or
| 3 demurrer. The court may likewise, in its discretion, after notice to the adverse
4 party, allow, upon any terms as may be just, an amendment to any pleading or
proceeding in other particulars; and may upon like terms allow an answer to be
5 made after the time limits by this code.”
6 Because the University already filed its answer to the Complaint, and the case is “at
7 |} issue,” Plaintiff may not amend the complaint without leave of court. Decl. Leask, 95, Ex. B.
8 To do so, a formal motion to amend must be served and filed. Dye v. Catepillar, Inc.
9 ||(2011) 195 Ca.4th 1366, 1380. |
10 California Code of Civil Procedure section 1005(b) provides that: |
i “(b) Unless otherwise ordered or specifically provided by law, all moving and
supporting papers shall be served and filed at least 16 court days before the
12 hearing. The moving and supporting papers served shall be a copy of the papers
filed or to be filed with the court. However, if the notice is served by mail, the
13 required 16-day period of notice before the hearing shall be increased by five
calendar days if the place of mailing and the place of address are within the State
14 of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of
address is outside the State of California but within the United States, and 20
15 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the
United States, and if the notice is served by facsimile transmission, express mail,
16 or another method of delivery providing for overnight delivery, the required 16-
day period of notice before the hearing shall be increased by two calendar days.
17 Section 1013, which extends the time within which a right may be exercised or an
act may be done, does not apply to a notice of motion, papers opposing a motion,
18 or reply papers governed by this section. All papers opposing a motion so noticed
shall be filed with the court and a copy served on each party at least nine court
19 days, and all reply papers at least five court days before the hearing.”
20 As indicated above, Plaintiff filed his motion on March 7, 2013, which is less than
21 ||sixteen (16) court days before the date on which this motion will be heard by the Court. (Decl.
| 22 || Leask, §{11-12, Ex. D.) Thus, Plaintiff failed to provide sufficient notice of this motion and the
| 23 || motion should be denied.
24 Moreover, pursuant to California Rules of Court, the motion must include a copy of the
25 || proposed amendment; identify by page, paragraph, and line number any additions to and
26 || deletions from the prior pleading; and be supported by a memorandum of points and authorities
27 j/and a declaration specifying the purpose and effect of the proposed amendments, why the
28 |i proposed amendments are necessary and proper; when the facts giving rise to the proposed
BANGOURA’S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT
S:Client\101 USF\050 Bangoura SFSC\Pleadings\Pif's Second Motion for Leave to Amend\FINAL Opposition MT Amend Complaint.doc -4-

 
1 ||amendment were discovered; and the reasons why the request for amendment was not made
2 |jearlier. See California Rules of Court, Rules 3.1324, 3.1113. |
3 As indicated above, Plaintiff's motion does not specify the purpose and effect of the
4 || proposed amendments, why the proposed amendments are necessary and proper; when the facts |
5 || giving rise to the proposed amendment were discovered; and the reasons why the request for |
6 || amendment was not made earlier. (Decl. Leask, 7913-14, Ex. D.)
7 Plaintiff thus failed entirely to comply with California Rules of Court and for this |
8 || additional reason, the motion should be denied.
9 B. Alternatively, Plaintiff’s Proposed Amended Pleading Does Not Allege |
Any Substantive Changes, Nor Is There Any Reason To Explain The |
10 Need For The Amendment, Both Of Which Warrant The Exercise Of |
1 The Court’s Discretion To Deny The Motion
12 As indicated above, there are no substantive changes to causes of action or damages
13 || alleged (if any) in Plaintiff's proposed FAC. Plaintiff alleges no new material facts or any legal
14 || theories upon which he could maintain his causes of action in his proposed FAC. (Decl. Leask,
15 || 9915-20, Ex. D.)
16 Further, Plaintiff makes no change in the damages that he claims. (Decl. Leask, §21, Ex.
| 17 ||D.)
| 18 Accordingly, providing Plaintiff with leave to amend his complaint would be futile, as
19 || there is no need for the amendment.
20 For plaintiffs who wait until after the opposing party has filed a motion for summary
21 ||judgment, that delay is itself good cause to deny to amend. Fisher v. Larsen (1982) 138
22 || Cal.App.3d 627:
23 “The Motion to Amend After Summary Judgment Was Properly Denied.
24 Fisher knew of the motions for summary judgment as early as January 6, 1981,
but took no action to amend for five months. The rights to amend a complaint
25 rest within the sound discretion of the court (Robinson & Wilson, Inc. v. Stone
| (1973) 35 Cal.App.3d 396, 413 [110 Cal.Rptr. 675]), and may be denied where
26 there has been a long delay in seeking the amendment. (Rainer v. Community
| Memorial Hosp. (1971) 18 Cal.App.3d 240, 258 [95 Cal.Rptr. 901].) The court
| 27 was within its discretion in denying the amendment.” Jd. at 649.
28 HW
| BANGOURA v. USF [SFSC CGC-12-518990|— USF’S MPA IN OPPOSITION TO PLAINTIFF SORIBA
| BANGOURA’S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT
S:AClient\101 USF\050 Bangoura SFSC\Pleadings\PIfs Second Motion for Leave to Amend\FINAL Opposition MT Amend Complaint.doc -5-

 
eee
1 Here, the length of delay was over one year after Plaintiff filed his original Complaint
2 || and over seven weeks after the University filed its motion for summary judgment. (Decl. Leask, |
3 || 994-11, Exs. A, D.)
4 Such delay is independent cause for denial of the motion. Yee v. Mobilehome Park |
5 || Rental Review Board (1998) 62 Cal.App.4th 1409:
6 “The last question is whether the Yees were improperly denied their right to
amend the petition to state causes of action .... The Yees argue the proposed
7 amendments were ‘tendered early within the damage phase of the case and in a |
3 manner which would be totally nonprejudicial to the [Board].’” |
“The Yees ignore the facts that (a) the constitutional claims are (as we have held,
9 ante) barred in any event, and (b) the proposed amendments were offered more
than two years after the original complaint was filed, and shortly before a final
10 resolution of all of the issues remaining before the superior court. As another |
court held in like circumstances, "[O]ur .. . review of the record reveals no abuse |
1] of discretion in the denial of the request for leave to amend. [The] amendment |
was proposed on the eve of trial, nearly two years after the complaint was |
12 originally filed. [The Yees] did not give an explanation for leaving the .. . |
claim[s] out of the original complaint or bringing the request to amend so late. 'A |
13 party who waits 18 months before attempting to amend, and then does so only |
after trial has commenced, and who offers no excuse for the failure, can hardly
14 complain when the request to amend is denied.' [Citation.]" (Magpali v. Farmers |
Group, Inc., supra, 48 Cal. App. 4th at p. 486; see also Stevenson v. San |
15 Francisco Housing Authority (1994) 24 Cal. App. 4th 269, 284 [29 Cal. Rptr. 2d |
398] ["We find no error in the denial of appellant's request to amend after having |
16 seven previous chances to state a cause of action."].)” Jd. at 1428-1429 |
17 Plaintiff, like the Yee’s, waited long to move to amend and like the Yee’s, offered no
18 || explanation for his delay. Id.
19 “When a trial court denies leave to amend, the decision has been upheld on
| grounds such as the fact that the amendment contained objectionable subject
| 20 matter or because of the conduct of the moving party or belated presentation of
the amendment. (See Dos Pueblos Ranch & Imp. Co. v. Ellis (1937) 8 Cal.2d 617,
21 622 [67 P.2d 340]; Ross v. McDougal (1939) 31 Cal. App. 2d 114, 121 [87 P.2d
| » 709].)” Atkinson v. Elk Corporation (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 739, 759.
|
23 That Plaintiff failed to explain why the motion is necessary and his failure to explain
24 || why he waited until the eve of the hearing on the motion for summary judgment, also warrants
25 || denial of this motion.
26 Furthermore, as explained above, Plaintiff's moving papers entirely fail to comply with
27 ||Rules of Court, in that they offer no explanation whatsoever for the delay. (Decl. Leask, 4]13-
28 4/14, Ex. D.)
BANGOURA vy. USF [SFSC CGC-12-518990]— USF’S MPA IN OPPOSITION TO PLAINTIFF SORIBA
BANGOURA’S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT
SAClient\101 USF\050 Bangoura SFSC\Pleadings\P!f's Second Motion for Leave to Amend\FINAL Opposition MT Amend Complaint.doc -6-

 
| © @
| 1 For these additional reasons, the motion should be denied.
2 IV. CONCLUSION
3 Based on the foregoing, the University requests that the Court deny Plaintiff's motion in
4 || its entirety.
5
